title: Commercial Roofing in Waverly, MI | Commercial Roofing Contractors of Lansing
metaDescription: Roofing for Waverly's west-Lansing retail corridors and office buildings along Saginaw Highway and Waverly Road, scoped for Michigan winter and wind exposure.

html:
Waverly Road Retail and Office Roofs West of Downtown Lansing

Waverly sits on Lansing's west side, where Saginaw Highway and Waverly Road carry a steady mix of retail plazas, medical office buildings, and freestanding commercial buildings, most of it built out over three or four decades of incremental development. Most of these roofs are low-slope membrane over strip-mall or single-tenant construction, which means the same handful of failure points show up again and again once a roof passes fifteen or twenty years old.

Strip Retail Roofs Share More Problems Than They Should

A lot of the Waverly Road commercial stock was built in phases as the corridor developed, which means neighboring units often carry roofs from different eras patched together at party walls. We've walked plazas where one bay has a ten-year-old TPO recover and the unit next door still has the original built-up roof from the 1980s. Water doesn't stop at the property line between tenants, so a drainage problem two doors down can still show up as a stain in a unit that has a newer roof.

Because of that, we look at the whole roof plane during an estimate rather than only the leased square footage of the tenant who called. Roof age, drainage slope, and prior repair history on the adjoining bays all factor into what we recommend, because a fix that ignores the neighboring roof section is a fix that doesn't hold. An owner who only fixes their own unit's roof without checking what the neighboring bay is doing to drainage across the plaza is buying a repeat call.

Office Buildings Carry a Different Set of Rooftop Equipment

The medical and professional office buildings along this corridor tend to carry more rooftop mechanical equipment per square foot than the retail plazas do — multiple RTUs, exhaust fans, and sometimes emergency generators, each one a penetration that has to be flashed and maintained. Roof traffic for equipment service is a bigger factor here than on a typical retail box, and we build walkway pads and curb detailing into any reroof scope so the membrane isn't taking foot traffic damage every time an HVAC tech services a unit. Generator pads, satellite mounts, and security camera masts add still more penetrations on some of these office buildings, and each one gets logged on the roof plan so a future service call starts with a map instead of a guess.

West-Side Wind and Freeze-Thaw Exposure

Waverly's open commercial parcels along Saginaw Highway don't have much windbreak from taller buildings, and wind loading on parapets and rooftop equipment shows up here more than it does on tighter downtown blocks. Combined with mid-Michigan's freeze-thaw winters, that's a two-front problem: wind works loose edge metal and equipment curbs, and freeze-thaw cycling opens the seams and sealant joints that wind exposure already stressed.

Spring meltwater finds every one of those openings at once. We see the pattern every year on these corridor roofs: a fall inspection that gets skipped means a spring service call for a leak that was actually caused by a January freeze cycle, not anything that happened during the storm the tenant blames it on.

What We Check First on a Waverly Corridor Roof

  • Party-wall drainage between attached retail units, checked across the full plaza
  • RTU curb flashing and equipment pad condition on office buildings
  • Edge metal and parapet cap attachment after wind events
  • Seam and lap condition on membranes past the midpoint of their warranty term
  • Roof age and system mismatch between adjoining tenant spaces
  • Standing water at low points, especially near mechanical wells

What Waiting on a Waverly Roof Actually Costs an Owner

Strip retail and office landlords along this corridor usually face the same pressure: a leak that's tenant-facing gets fixed fast, but the roof underneath it doesn't get evaluated as a whole system until it fails again. That approach adds up. A patch-only strategy on an aging built-up roof typically costs more over five years than a planned recover would have, and it comes with tenant complaints and potential lease disputes attached every time it happens.

We price replacement and recover options side by side against a patch-and-wait approach so a landlord can see the real five-year cost difference rather than only the invoice for today's leak. For buildings where the roof is a shared asset across multiple tenants, we also flag when a CAM-reimbursable capital project makes more sense than another round of individually billed repairs.

Waverly Corridor Roofing Questions

Can you inspect a multi-tenant roof without notifying every tenant?

We coordinate through the property manager or landlord, and we can usually access the roof without disrupting individual tenant spaces below.

Do you handle roofs with mismatched systems between units?

Yes, that's common on this corridor. We evaluate the whole roof plane and note where transitions between systems are creating drainage or flashing problems.

What's the typical membrane choice for a Waverly office building?

TPO and EPDM both see use here; see our TPO single-ply roofing page for how we weigh that decision against a building's equipment load and budget.

How often should a corridor retail roof be inspected?

Twice a year at minimum — before winter and after it — given how much freeze-thaw and wind exposure this stretch of Saginaw Highway sees.

Can roof work be scheduled around retail business hours?

Yes, most exterior roof work doesn't require closing a storefront, and we coordinate loud or disruptive tasks around the tenant's peak hours where possible.
